metadata.title: Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses

abstract: Responses to open-ended items (OEI) in surveys in evaluation research are valuable but often short or even missing. We investigate how response behavior can be altered by means of survey design. In a survey on tutoring courses, 3,183 students responded to OEI. Participants were randomly assigned to conditions with or without a motivational cue and with OEI positioned at the beginning or the end of the survey, respectively. We simultaneously inspected response rates and answer lengths using Bayesian multilevel hurdle models. Positioning at the beginning enhanced response rates but reduced answer lengths. The motivational cue raised response rates only at the end of the survey. We also applied two different topic modeling approaches. Resulting topics cover different aspects of teacher behavior and positioning slightly altered the content of the responses. Based on motivational and cognitive theories, we address both the design of questionnaires and analysis approaches to OEI responses.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Open-ended items (OEI) are frequently used and may provide meaningful insights, for example, in evaluation surveys alongside to closed questions (Marshall, 2021). These items are also referred to by terms such as open answers (Tashakkori et al., 2021), and "leave the respondent to decide the wording of the answer, the length of the answer, and the kind of matters to be raised in the answer" (Denscombe, 2017, p. 194). Respondents may raise additional points and may also perceive greater autonomy (Denscombe, 2017). Readers and recipients frequently prefer OEI responses as more illustrative and easier to interpret in comparison to results from closed questions (Fjørtoft & Lai, 2021).

However, OEI come along with two major drawbacks: (1) They are perceived more difficult to answer by respondents, thus yielding higher nonresponse rates (Denscombe, 2017;Schmidt et al., 2020). This may result in biased subsamples of participants responding to these questions and a low representativeness of the obtained responses (Denscombe, 2017;Galesic, 2006;Holland & Christian, 2009;McCord, 2013). (2) OEI responses are usually short, unstructured text chunks and not straightforward to analyze. As a consequence, despite the narrative nature of the responses, interpreting them properly may be challenging (Denscombe, 2017;O'Cathain & Thomas, 2004) and qualitative methods usually applied to longer text segments are timeconsuming and may not be appropriate (LaDonna et al., 2018;O'Cathain & Thomas, 2004). Therefore, the use of text mining approaches is a promising alternative (Gencoglu et al., 2023;Hujala et al., 2020). However, text mining is an evolving field with a growing number of approaches (Kwartler, 2017), and approaches may elicit different aspects within relatively rich responses to OEI (Gavras et al., 2022). Theoretical considerations on motivational and cognitive processes underlying answer behavior may guide both study design and data analysis to develop strategies for designing surveys.

Below, we outline motivational and cognitive models underlying answer behavior as a theoretical basis and review empirical findings on how to improve answer quality of OEI. In a randomized field experiment, we investigate the impact of a motivational cue and position effects on response behavior in a survey for student evaluation of teaching. We further employ different text mining approaches to analyze different hypotheses based on the motivational and cognitive theories.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: For response motivation, a widely applied model by Krosnick (1991Krosnick ( , 1999) ) distinguishes motivational states described as optimizing and satisficing: Optimizing occurs when respondents conduct necessary cognitive tasks for item response properly. Satisficing means that these processes are not executed profoundly (weak satisficing) or may not even be executed at all (strong satisficing). For OEI, weak satisficing may result in responses to OEI of lower quality, while strong satisficing may result in missing responses (Schmidt et al., 2020). Satisficing can be regarded to result from both survey and respondents characteristics, and becomes more likely with growing task difficulty and lower respondents' ability (Krosnick, 1999).

Process models on motivation such as the Rubicon model (Achtziger & Gollwitzer, 2018;Heckhausen & Gollwitzer, 1987) distinguish different phases within the course of action, especially a predecisional motivational phase of information seeking in contrast to volitional phases of preparing, conducting, and evaluating actions. The steps of this model can be mapped to strong satisficing (predecisional abort) versus weak satisficing and optimizing (entering volitional phase on different levels).

Although motivation does depend on person characteristics, it can be addressed, for example, by motivational cues stressing the importance of OEI and raising response rates and quality (Smyth et al., 2009;Zuell et al., 2015). In low stakes assessments, Liu et al. (2015) found beneficial effects of motivational cues on several motivation-related outcomes, indicating that these cues primarily work on a motivational basis.

Additionally, although OEI are usually positioned at the end of the questionnaire (Marshall, 2021), some studies suggest that asking these questions earlier might be beneficial. Schmidt et al. (2020) argue that motivation declines during the answering process. They compared position effects of OEI across several surveys on political issues and found that OEI asked earlier in the survey received longer and better interpretable answers and were answered faster, indicating a higher response motivation. In a study randomizing participants to differing questionnaire lengths, Galesic and Bosnjak (2009) found shorter responses to OEI when the respective items were asked repeatedly. This might be interpreted as a decline in motivation, but also a tendency not to repeat oneself. In another study, Galesic (2006) argues that participants perceive both lengthy surveys and question blocks containing OEI as burdensome undermining the respondents' motivation. Instead of placing burdensome OEI at the end of a questionnaire, asking OEI at the beginning might benefit from higher motivation of respondents.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Concerning cognition, a well-known model by Groves et al. (2009), on the response process distinguishes four steps for answering a survey item: (1) reading and comprehending the question, (2) recalling necessary information, (3) estimation and judgment based on this information, and ( 4) reporting the answer. This process can be facilitated by previous activation of information necessary to answer an item and is related to item order and context effects (cf. Schwarz et al., 1992). For OEI, Schmidt et al. (2020) found more detailed responses concerning political issues when these items were asked ahead of elections, and Geer (1991) further found that salient information was more frequently reported when additionally provided before responding to OEI.

These findings suggest that it might be beneficial to position OEI at the end of the survey: During answering closed questions, information may have been already activated, and responses to OEI may be more elaborated. As the response process is complex (Schwarz et al., 1992), it remains open if the responses to OEI do resemble or rather deviate from the content of the formerly asked closed items.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: From the description above, it becomes clear that motivational and cognitive processes are intertwined. For example, in the motivational phase of the Rubicon model, information on the task is processed to decide if and how to engage in it, while later on cognitive processes are used to answer items. Additionally, person characteristics and respondents' attitude toward the object of evaluation may be relevant predictors for answer behavior (Holland & Christian, 2009;Marshall, 2021;Schmidt et al., 2020;Zuell et al., 2015). However, aspects of survey design are easier to manipulate in both empirical studies and when creating surveys. We focus on the use of motivational cues as a well-known technique to increase response qualityprimarily related to motivational processesand on positioning of OEI as a less researched topic more connected to cognitive aspects.

Although experimental manipulation regarding survey design is promising, the difficulty of adequately analyzing short text responses to OEI remains (O'Cathain & Thomas, 2004). Schmidt et al. (2020) recommend to select and combine different response features and text mining on theoretical considerations. Possible response features include response rates (Holland & Christian, 2009;Marshall, 2021;Zuell et al., 2015), answer lengths (Gavras et al., 2022;Holland & Christian, 2009;Mahon-Haft & Dillman, 2010;Schmidt et al., 2020), response and latency times (Schmidt et al., 2020;Smyth et al., 2009), answer tone (Gavras et al., 2022;Mahon-Haft & Dillman, 2010), number of mentioned topics (Gavras et al., 2022;Holland & Christian, 2009;Mahon-Haft & Dillman, 2010;Smyth et al., 2009), and interpretability or elaboration of answers (Schmidt et al., 2020;Smyth et al., 2009). For most features, different analysis approaches are possible (e.g., using character or word count for answer lengths, or different approaches to extract topics). Additionally, different response features might reflect motivational (e.g., response rates) or cognitive (e.g., topics) processes to a differing degree or may represent even both (e.g., answer length). By combining experimental manipulation of survey features with the selection of text mining approaches, our central goal is to gain a deeper understanding of motivational and cognitive processes underlying answer behavior for OEI.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Both theoretical models and empirical findings suggest that response behavior to OEI may be altered by questionnaire design, for example, by motivational cues or item positioning. Motivational and cognitive processes within respondents provide a link between questionnaire design and response behavior, and also imply specific features of the obtained responses. Table 1 summarizes the central hypotheses.

We expect that a motivational cue raises response rates due to increased motivation (Smyth et al., 2009;Zuell et al., 2015), but we expect no or only a minor impact on answer lengths and content. Concerning the positioning, we expect higher response rates for OEI positioned at the beginning of the questionnaire due to higher motivation. However, positioning at the end may lead to longer answers and also a thematic shift in the answers due to prior elaboration on the closed items (Galesic & Bosnjak, 2009;Schwarz et al., 1992).

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: The present experiment was conducted in the context of tutoring for the Abitur examination between October 2017 and February 2018 in several German cities (see Merk et al., 2019). Participants agreed to an informed consent during registration and filled in the survey on teacher and course feedback after the fourth day of their five days lasting course. Participants used their own mobile phones for the survey and were given course-specific short links leading to a customized survey, which mentioned the name of the respective teacher in the introduction and some items. Students were randomly assigned to different conditions of this 2 × 2 field trial, where OEI on positive and negative feedback were positioned at the beginning and at the end of the survey and accompanied by a motivational or no additional cue.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: A slightly adapted version of the SEEQ (Students' Evaluations of Educational Quality; Marsh, 1982) was applied consisting of 28 Likert type items capturing the instructional quality of the course on differing dimensions (e.g., self-reported learning results and interest, teacher behavior and interaction with students, clarity of explanations) and additional 14 items on the participants' demographic and school-related information as well as general satisfaction with the course, reasons for registering in it, and feedback to the course provider (see Merk et al., 2019, and the documentation of analysis in PsychArchives for a detailed overview on questions). Two further OEI asked for feedback on the teacher: "What has TEACH-ERNAME done especially well?" and "What could TEACHERNAME do better in future?" We refer to these items as positive feedback versus negative feedback later.

The sample itself consisted of 3,183 students attending 287 courses held by 106 teachers. Of those 3,183 students, 788 students received the OEI at the beginning of the survey with the motivating instruction ("The following questions are very important for TEACHER NAME and SCHOOL NAME and will help us to better respond to your wishes as students in the future"), 775 at the beginning with the neutral instruction, 816 at the end with the motivating instruction, and 804 at the end with the neutral instruction. Concerning the Likert type SEEQ items, we found very low nonresponse rates up to 54 persons (<2%) not responding on single items. Answers to open questions consisted of single words such as "structure" or "Nothing," short expressions such as "Explained in detail" or "He is doing great," and up to multiple sentences raising different points such as "He responded precisely to the needs of the course. He was able to answer questions precisely and guide you to the right solution." or "I thought it was particularly good that she responded to every question. And she always tried to explain something so that everyone understood it and she had no problem not even repeating it."

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: On surface characteristics of OEI responses, we report descriptive statistics and probabilistic models. Since we theoretically assume that the text length is determined by two separate processes (motivational processes guiding the decision to answer the item, and volitional-cognitive processes on obtaining and formulating an answer), we considered this assumption using a so-called hurdle regression model (Mullahy, 1986). Hurdle models use different and independent submodels for processes leading to zeros (e.g., logistic regression model) and processes leading to any other count (e.g., Poisson regression). As we faced nested data (e.g., students nested in classes), we used multilevel versions of these models (Zuur et al., 2009). To be able to also generate evidence for null effects, we chose a Bayesian estimation procedure using the R package brms (Bürkner, 2017). We fitted a baseline model, which only contains a dummy predictor for the OEI and random intercepts for class and teacher and a model incorporating coefficients for both experimental factors. Additionally, as the descriptive analyses suggested an interaction for the experimental factors with regard to response rates, we specified a third model containing this interaction for the hurdle and the negative binomial part. We report odds ratios (OR) and incidence ratios as effect sizes for every regression coefficient along to their credibility intervals.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: We leveraged two different approaches for topic modelingstructural topic modeling (STM; Roberts et al., 2014) as an established approach, and BERTopic (Grootendorst, 2022) using text embeddings. We applied both approaches to positive and negative feedback separately, which leads to four different lines in analysis. We did not distinguish between experimental conditions during topic extraction and labeling. In an additional step, we identified which topics were mentioned most frequently within each experimental condition, respectively. Note that this procedure differs from other approaches (e.g., Gavras et al., 2022) and focuses on prevalence of topic in the conditions, but not on the number of topics mentioned in each experimental condition.

Concerning STM, after preprocessing the data (removing punctuation, numbers, stop words, and symbols) with the package quanteda (Benoit et al., 2018), we used the stm package (Roberts et al., 2019) for the final analysis. We ran analyses for different numbers of topics ranging from 3 to 30. Based on different metrics (residuals, coherence, and exclusiveness; see Weston et al., 2023), we favored some solutions and discussed possible interpretations of the resulting topics in our team. For labeling, we used both typical terms for each topic and exemplary answers as a basis and discussed different labels in an interdisciplinary team. Details on determining the number of topics can be retrieved from the documentation of analysis in PsychArchives. We decided to use 11 topics for positive feedback and 12 topics for negative feedback. In a further step, the first and last authors decided on the final label. When we perceived a lower content-related coherence, we added Mixed to the label. Finally, for determining the occurrence of the different topics in the conditions and as STM assumes a mixture of topics in each answer (Maier et al., 2018), we first extracted the most prominent topic per answer. Afterward, we calculated the proportion of the different topics within each experimental condition and displayed these proportions graphically.

Current approaches using word embeddings from pretrained models may yield additional insights into the themes underlying the answers. A quite flexible and powerful approach is the BERTopic package (Grootendorst, 2022), which, for example, has been recommended over LDAbased approaches similar to STM for analyzing Twitter posts (Egger & Yu, 2022) and also supports German language within the analyzed data. This approach leverages word embeddings from BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) and uses dimension reduction and clustering techniques for detecting topics. As responses containing stop words resulted in major topics referring to gender of the teacher, we excluded stop words during the analysis following the recommendations of author of the package. For displaying results, we grouped topics with few associated responses. Finally, and analogously to STM, we labeled the resulting topics, calculated the proportion of resulting topics per experimental condition, and visualized topic proportions for these four different conditions.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Descriptive statistics on response rates and answer lengths showed a high over all response rate of 78.2% and rightskewed answer lengths (M = 79.6, Mdn = 59, IQR = 104; see Figure 1). Figure 1 displays response rates (Figure 1A) and answer lengths (Figure 1B) in the different experimental conditions. Table 2 provides the corresponding results from the multilevel negative-binomial hurdle model. Here, larger coefficients of the hurdle part indicate a larger barrier to provide answer and correspond to lower response rates while coefficients for the negative-binomial part directly resemble answer lengths.

Response rates benefited from positioning items at the beginning of the survey (left-hand side vs. right-hand side of Figure 1A). This is also reflected by the odds ratio of the dummy coded predictor for the position I position at the end in the hurdle part of the model: Its point estimation is 4.3 (see Table 2), whereas the credibility interval (CI) is completely outside the region of practical null equivalence (ROPE; Kruschke, 2018), which we defined for this study as the range from a small negative effect to a small positive effect (0.60 < OR < 1.68; Chen et al., 2010). This means we can reject the null hypothesis of a small effect or null effect of positioning on nonresponse. Positioning also affected answer length. Figure 1B already suggests that this may be a tiny effect, which was confirmed by an incidence ratio of 1.12 (CI = [1.07, 1.19]) in the negative-binomial part of the model (see Table 2).

Predicting nonresponse and answer length with dummy coded predictors for the motivational cue I position at the end resulted in evidence for null effects (hurdle part: OR = 0.91, CI = [0.67, 1.24]; negative-binomial part: OR = 0.98, CI = [0.93, 1.03]). This again is in line with Figure 1, as marginalized over the position no difference per motivational cue is (visually) detectable.

However, Figure 1 implies an interaction between positioning and the motivating instruction: While the motivational cue raises response rates at the end of the survey (69.1% to 75.9%, right-hand side of Figure 1A), it diminishes response rates at the beginning (86.4% to 81.5%, left-hand side of Figure 1A). The CI of the corresponding predictor is completely outside the ROPE for the hurdle part of the model (OR = 0.22, CI = [0.12, 0.41]) but completely inside the ROPE for the negative-binomial part (OR = 1.01, CI = [0.91, 1.13]). Hence, the inferential statistic provides strong evidence for at least small interaction effect of position and motivational cue for nonresponse, but also strong evidence for a null interaction effect of position and motivational cue for answer length.

This is also reflected by the odds ratio of the dummy coded predictor for the position I position at the end in the hurdle part of the model: Its point estimation is 4.3 (see Table 2), whereas the credibility interval (CI) is completely outside the region of practical null equivalence (ROPE; Kruschke, 2018), which we defined for this study as the range from a small negative effect to a small positive effect (0.60 < OR < 1.68; Chen et al., 2010). This means we can reject the null hypothesis of a small effect or null effect of positioning on nonresponse. Positioning also affected answer length. Figure 1B already suggests that this may be a tiny effect, which was confirmed by an incidence ratio of 1.12 (CI = [1.07, 1.19]) in the negative-binomial part of the model (see Table 2).

Predicting nonresponse and answer length with dummy coded predictors for the motivational cue I position at the end resulted in evidence for null effects (hurdle part: OR = 0.91, CI = [0.67, 1.24]; negative-binomial part: OR = 0.98, CI = [0.93, 1.03]). This again is in line with Figure 1, as marginalized over the position no difference per motivational cue is (visually) detectable.

However, Figure 1 implies an interaction between positioning and the motivating instruction: While the motivational cue raises response rates at the end of the survey (69.1% to 75.9%, right-hand side of Figure 1A), it diminishes response rates at the beginning (86.4% to 81.5%, left-hand side of Figure 1A). The CI of the corresponding predictor is completely outside the ROPE for the hurdle part of the model (OR = 0.22, CI = [0.12, 0.41]) but completely inside the ROPE for the negative-binomial part (OR = 1.01, CI = [0.91, 1.13]). Hence, the inferential statistic provides strong evidence for at least small interaction effect of position and motivational cue for nonresponse, but also strong evidence for a null interaction effect of position and motivational cue for answer length.

paragraphs: Concerning the content of responses to OEI, we used two different topic modeling approaches, STM and an embedding-based approach leveraging BERT. Figure 2 displays the topics detected in the positive and negative feedback for both approaches and the relative frequencies of these topics within each experimental condition.

Concerning STM, we inspected several numbers of resulting topics. For responses concerning positive feedback (Figure 2A), we report the solution with 11 topics covering different aspects of teaching and explaining (e.g., Illustrative presentation and Good explanations on comprehensibility, Individualization if individual needs of students were addressed), as well as the atmosphere of the course and personal characteristics of the teacher (e.g., Friendly teacher, Enthusiasm, Positive role model). Concerning negative feedback (Figure 2B), the 12 reported topics were not that clear. They cover similar aspects ranging from suggestions to improve explanation quality in Explanation pace and Explanation depth, specific teaching strategies like to use exercises and tasks adequately in Task culture and Pacing exercises, individual wishes on a Different scope concerning the focus of the course, up to suggestions on a personal level like to improve the teacher's Entrance and appearance. There were also some topics that were difficult to label, for example, one category consisting of the demand for More . . . (e.g., "more examples," but also "more chocolate").

Concerning the experimental groups, we see the largest differences in some topics from positive feedback, with Good explanations and Individualization more frequently mentioned at the beginning of the survey, and course climate (Atmosphere) and the teacher's personal characteristics (Friendly teacher, Positive role model) more frequently mentioned at the end of the survey. In negative feedback responses, group differences are less pronounced, but again personal characteristics of the teacher (Entrance and appearance) more frequently mentioned at the end of the end of the questionnaire, a targeted use of exercises (Task culture) rather at the beginning.

For BERTopic, we found great shares of responses that were detected as outliers and were not clustered into one topic. Some aspects from STM repeated (e.g., relevance of explanations in different topics such as Good explanations and Explains friendly, support for students in Explains questions and Supporting, teacher characteristics and course climate in Motivating, Humorous). Some topics were mentioned more specifically (e.g., visual aids such as blackboard usage as an aspect of illustrative explanations) and short responses often indicating satisfaction with the course entered the results additionally. Concerning group differences, similar patterns such as in the STM approach occurred (e.g., Explaining questions was mentioned more frequently at the beginning, topics on course climate rather in the end of the survey) but are less pronounced than in the STM results.

Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Descriptive results (Figure 1A) and results from Bayesian multilevel modeling (Table 1) indicate rather high response rates to OEI in our sample when compared to other studies (Holland & Christian, 2009;McCord, 2013;O'Cathain & Thomas, 2004). We found a clear benefit of Note. Odds ratios, incidence ratios, and their credibility intervals of the Bayesian multilevel negative binomial hurdle models; I = indicator variable for the respective effects; ELPD = expected log-predictive density.

positioning OEI at the beginning of the surveys in terms of response rates. To our knowledge, this combination has not been investigated by previous studies. The motivational cue increased response rates when items were positioned at the end of the survey (Smyth et al., 2009;Zuell et al., 2015). However, this effect reversed for OEI positioned at the beginning of the survey. Similarly, Smyth et al. (2009) found no or reversed effects for motivating instructions in some cases. They argue that characteristics of items and of respondents might interfere in these cases (see O'Cathain & Thomas, 2004;Schmidt et al., 2020, for similar statements). Schwarz et al. (1992) stress that response behavior is a result of complex interactions between several features of survey design and person characteristics such as response motivation, ability, and attitude toward the evaluation object, and that contradictory findings may result from these interactions. This similarly applies to answer lengths as a second surface characteristic (Figure 1B and Table 1): We did not find an effect of the motivational cue provided in other studies (Smyth et al., 2009), but we observed positive effects of positioning the item at the end of the survey. This is in line with theoretical considerations on cognitive elaboration (Schwarz et al., 1992) but contradicts findings from Galesic and Bosnjak (2009) and Schmidt et al. (2020). However, Galesic and Bosnjak (2009) asked similar questions repeatedly, probably leading to fatigue and not wanting to repeat previous responses. Schmidt et al. (2020) addressed a subject only loosely related to the remaining questions. Thus, cognitive elaboration may have been undermined by a shift in content. However, these authors find that answer quality including length also increased when the questions were asked shortly before political elections, when it can be assumed that knowledge about the political topics was more easily accessible, as these topics were discussed in the media, for example.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Regarding the results from topic modeling approaches (Figure 2), respondents mentioned a wide range of topics. In general, explanations played a crucial role within many answers, but according to STM (Figure 2A and B), responses also included course climate, general and individual support and interaction with the students, and the teachers' behavioral and personal characteristics. Similar topics were also detected in students' evaluation of teaching using STM and similar approaches in other samples (Gencoglu et al., 2023;Hujala et al., 2020). Differences between these studies may probably result from different samples and teaching settings as well as the granularity of the detected topics. We also observed differences between results from STM and BERTopic in the current sample, where BERTopic additionally revealed topics that indicated general satisfaction of the course and topics more related to concrete characteristics and observable behavior such as being humorous or the use of visual aids. We similarly noted that topics were often closer related to word usage and easier to label. These observations could be a feature of the analysis approach that uses general embeddings without further fine-tuning to the task and the removal of stop words at a later stage, allowing short answers such as "Nothing." to enter the analysis process.

For experimental groups, we found differences in some topics: Participants answering OEI in the beginning mentioned explanations and individual support by the teacher more frequently, while participants answering OEI at the end mentioned rather mentioned course climate and associated issues such as a friendly, humorous, motivating teaching style. The latter aspects correspond to the first two factors of the SEEQ (Marsh, 1982) and therefore point to a possible primacy effect and that answers might be impacted by previous elaborations (Krosnick, 1999). However, we found very similar topics across experimental conditions, which makes us assume that the position does only have a minor impact on content in the present study. Nonetheless, topic overlap in OEI answers may differ between surveys (cf. Gavras et al., 2022, in general, andfor similar underlying data Gencoglu et al., 2023;Hujala et al., 2020), and we would like to emphasize the exploratory approach of the present analysis.

sections.header_raw: Limitations and Reflection of Our Approach
sections.header_norm: limitations and reflection of our approach
paragraphs: Sample and Procedure The present study was conducted as a field experiment using a relatively large sample from many German cities. As a field experiment, it provides a high ecological validity and internal validity. However, data were sampled in a specific context (tutoring lessons) with an obviously highly motivated sample, which limits generalizability of our results to other settings. Although the sample is quite large compared to other evaluations, it is rather small for machine learning and text mining approaches. We further selected analysis approaches based on theoretical considerations and in line with experimental design of the study. However, using several approaches to address specific points might be useful. For example, regarding the elaboration of answers, a closer look at lexical diversity of answers (Horbach & Zesch, 2019) or the number of mentioned topics (Gavras et al., 2022) might be additional indicators. Also further areas for text mining would be interesting, for example, considering sentiment of answers (e.g., like in Gavras et al., 2022) between positive and negative feedback or correlations between topics or sentiment in OEI responses and ratings of closed items (Hujala et al., 2020).

paragraphs: The Bayesian modeling approach on surface characteristics is able to mimic specific theoretical assumptions (the Rubicon model suggesting the use of a hurdle parameter) and data inherent aspects (multilevel structure) aspects. Furthermore, it allows simultaneous inference on both response rates and answer lengths, which constitutes a significant improvement to previous studies. We assumed that the response rates correspond to motivational processes and answer lengths to cognitive processes triggered by different experimental manipulations, respectively. However, due to the interaction for response rates, the picture is less clear as we hoped and raises the question for replication and more detailed theory development.

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Concerning topic modeling, we applied two approaches differing in basic assumptions and algorithmic steps and made several decisions during analysis. For example, STM assumes a mixture of topics in documents (Kwartler, 2017;Maier et al., 2018), and BERTopic a single topic per document (Egger & Yu, 2022). A first decision was to use the complete corpus from each survey question as the starting point to detect topics. This deviates from other approaches applying topic modeling within each experimental setting (Gavras et al., 2022) but has the advantage that the exact number of topics does not matter too much for the subsequent analysis steps. STM and BER-Topic require further decisions during the analysis process (e.g., on the number of topics in STM or on different steps and selection of a specific run for BERTopic), which may lead to different extracted topics. We tried different possibilities and found similar topics across different solutions. However, especially if it is not possible to create training and test data sets, which may often be the case in data sets from psychological research, additional strategies such as robustness checks (Gavras et al., 2022;Maier et al., 2018) or approaches to navigate a multiverse of analytic methods (Dafflon et al., 2022) might be suitable. Furthermore, it could be useful to compare the available text data and the assumptions of the procedures: Twitter texts tend to follow one main theme and BERTopic revealed good results for them (Egger & Yu, 2022), while for texts covering several topics (e.g., scientific texts such as in Blazevic et al., 2023), STM assuming a mixture of themes in the documents might be adequate. Finally, topic modeling could be enhanced by zero-shot approaches (Schopf et al., 2022). However, the formulation of keywords needs some caution, and these models are still evolving (see the last part of the documentation of our analysis in PsychArchives for a first attempt). Nevertheless, these approaches might be a first step to developing a content-related theory and training models to accomplish specific tasks (e.g., Li et al., 2023), which also seems promising for implementation in automated analysis of answers in student evaluation of teaching (Hujala et al., 2020).

sections.paper_id: Hahn et al. - 2024 - Improving and Analyzing Open-Ended Survey Responses A Case Study Linking Psychological Theories and
paragraphs: Starting from a randomized experiment in an ecologically valid setting designed to investigate response behavior to OEI, we showed how psychological theories and different analytical approaches interact in this case study. Based on considerations from motivational and cognitive theories, we chose different analysis methods that focus on different features of text data, including Bayesian modeling of surface features and advanced text mining approaches. These approaches take advantage of the complexity of text responses to shed light on the underlying motivational and cognitive processes.
